title: Quantum game theory for 2 2 games: a mathematical framework
abstract: We develop a rigorous mathematical framework for quantum game theory applied to static 2x2 games, extending classical concepts to the quantum setting where players may employ arbitrary unitary operations (pure strategies) or probability measures over the continuous group SU(2) (mixed strategies). The Eisert-Wilkens-Lewenstein protocol is introduced as the standard implementation of quantum 2x2 games. We prove the existence of Nash equilibria for continuous quantum mixed strategies via a fixed-point argument, generalising the classical Nash theorem to the quantum case.
summary: This paper constructs a rigorous mathematical theory for quantum game theory applied to 2x2 games, extending classical concepts to the quantum domain using SU(2) unitaries. It proves the existence of quantum Nash equilibria for continuous mixed strategies and demonstrates that quantum strategies can yield payoffs superior to classical ones. The work is significant for understanding strategic interactions in quantum mechanical settings.
main_problem: The paper develops a rigorous mathematical framework to extend quantum game theory to static 2x2 games, specifically proving the existence of Nash equilibria for continuous quantum mixed strategies.
main_result: It proves the existence of quantum Nash equilibria for continuous mixed strategies using fixed-point theorems, showing that quantum strategies can outperform classical ones in certain scenarios like the Chicken game.
method: The analysis uses the Eisert-Wilkens-Lewenstein (EWL) protocol, representing strategies as unitary operations in SU(2) and defining mixed strategies as probability measures on SU(2).
model_or_system: The model is a static two-player 2x2 game analyzed in the quantum setting using qubits and unitary operators in SU(2). The framework compares quantum strategies to classical probability distributions.
key_observables: Expected payoffs for both players ($\langle A angle, \langle B angle$), which are derived as expectation values of payoff operators.
important_parameters: The entanglement parameter $\gamma$ in the EWL protocol, and the continuous strategy parameters defining the unitary operations in SU(2).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes players can manipulate qubits without introducing decoherence, and the existence proof relies on applying theorems like Kakutani-Glicksberg-Ky Fan to the space of measures M(SU(2)).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the mathematical formalism for quantum strategies and mixed states, then introduces the EWL protocol, and finally applies this framework to prove the existence of Nash equilibria and analyze payoff differences in specific games.
why_interesting: While not directly about AMO systems, the mathematical tools developed for analyzing optimal strategies in quantum games (using SU(2) representations and fixed-point theorems) are highly relevant for understanding optimal control or equilibrium states in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
